lesson.title: Reshaping Data
lesson.body: Data often needs reshaping between wide and long formats. pd.melt() converts wide to long (unpivots), df.pivot_table() converts long to wide (pivots). .stack() pivots columns to rows; .unstack() does the reverse. Wide format has one row per entity with multiple columns for time or categories; long format has one row per observation. Long format is preferred for most statistical analysis.

rooms.question: Complete the code to convert wide format to long format:
rooms.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'id':[1,2],'score_math':[80,90],'score_eng':[75,85]})
long = pd.___(df, id_vars=['id'], var_name='subject', value_name='score')
rooms.explanation: pd.melt() unpivots (wide → long): id_vars stay as identifier columns, var_name names the new category column, value_name names the value column.

rooms.question: Complete the code to create a pivot table of mean scores by student and subject:
rooms.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'student':['A','A','B','B'],'subject':['Math','Eng','Math','Eng'],'score':[80,75,90,85]})
table = df.pivot_table(values='score', index='student', columns='subject', aggfunc=___)
rooms.explanation: pivot_table() aggregates data: index=rows, columns=columns, values=what to aggregate, aggfunc=how. This is the pivot equivalent of a spreadsheet pivot table.

rooms.question: When should you use long format over wide format?
answers: For statistical analysis, machine learning, and plotting with libraries like seaborn
When the number of time periods is small
When each entity has exactly one observation
rooms.explanation: Long format (tidy data) is preferred for analysis: one observation per row, one variable per column. Most Python ML and stats libraries (seaborn, statsmodels) expect this format.

rooms.question: Order wide-to-long and long-to-wide operations correctly:
items: Wide format: one row per entity, multiple observation columns
Long format: one row per observation
rooms.explanation: Wide → melt → Long → pivot_table → Wide. The two operations are inverses of each other.

rooms.question: Complete the code to reset the index after groupby:
rooms.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'dept':['A','B','A'],'val':[1,2,3]})
result = df.groupby('dept').sum().___()
rooms.explanation: After groupby().sum(), the group key ('dept') becomes the index. .reset_index() promotes it back to a regular column, giving a flat DataFrame.

boss.name: The Reshape Demon
boss.taunt: Your data is wide where it should be long. Your MultiIndex confuses you.

questions.question: Complete the code to stack column levels into row index:
questions.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'A':[1,2],'B':[3,4]}, index=['x','y'])
stacked = df.___()
questions.explanation: df.stack() moves the innermost column level to become the innermost row level (creates a MultiIndex Series). df.unstack() is the inverse.

questions.question: A MultiIndex DataFrame can be accessed at the outer level with:
questions.explanation: df.loc['outer_key'] or df.xs('outer_key') select the outer level of a MultiIndex. Both work; xs() is more explicit about cross-section selection.

questions.question: Complete the code to add a new computed column:
questions.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'price':[100,200,150],'qty':[2,3,1]})
df['revenue'] = df['price'] ___ df['qty']
questions.explanation: Revenue = price × quantity. Pandas Series support element-wise arithmetic with *, +, -, / operators directly.
